text: In this case, we are describing laparoscopic repair for a patient having a grade III Cystocele. In an obese patient like this, use of a flange retractor to keep the bowels away is extremely beneficial. The initial part of the surgery is just like any other routine surgery where a subtotal hysterectomy is performed.

The surgeon dissects the uterovascular fold and opens the anterior space between the vagina and bladder. The dissection proceeds towards the bladder trigone, with the Foley's bulb inside the bladder aiding in identifying the correct plane. The dissection is performed smoothly and bloodlessly until the bladder trigone is reached, marking the lower limit of the dissection.

The surgeon pulls the bladder upward to facilitate the opening of the space with the cavitational effect of carbon dioxide, enhancing visibility and ease of laparoscopic dissection. A three-dimensional camera provides a detailed view of the deep surgical field.

The surgeon ligates and anchors the pre-cut mesh in the anterior space using non-absorbable esterless 3-0 sutures. Two anchor stitches are placed along the vaginal wall at the junction between the vagina, cervix, and bladder. Additional anchors are placed lower to stabilize the mesh, followed by stitches at the apex of the cervix or pubocervical ligament to ensure complete anchoring.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to pull the long arms of the mesh out of the abdominal cavity extraperitoneally, positioning them just outside the abdomen with artery forceps. The mesh arms are suspended to assess the adequate tension before peritoneal closure, ensuring the mesh remains extraperitoneal to avoid exposure to peritoneal contents.
The surgeon completes the hernia repair surgery by ensuring the mesh is fully extraperitonealized, achieving a bloodless outcome due to correct dissection planes.
